Front: What was the Ancien Régime?
Back: The political and social system in France before the Revolution

Front: Who ruled France before the Revolution?
Back: King Louis XVI

Front: What were the Three Estates?
Back: The clergy the nobility and the Third Estate

Front: What percentage of the population was the Third Estate?
Back: About 97 percent

Front: Why was the Third Estate dissatisfied?
Back: They paid most taxes but had little political power

Front: What financial crisis faced France in the 1780s?
Back: Huge national debt caused by wars and royal spending

Front: Which wars increased French debt?
Back: The Seven Years War and the American War of Independence

Front: Why did Louis XVI call the Estates General?
Back: To address the financial crisis

Front: When did the Estates General meet?
Why did the Estates General cause conflict?
Back: Voting was traditionally by estate rather than by head

Front: What was the National Assembly?
Back: A body formed by the Third Estate to represent the people

Front: What was the Tennis Court Oath?
Back: A pledge not to separate until a constitution was written

Front: When was the Tennis Court Oath?
Back: June 1789

Front: What was the Storming of the Bastille?
Back: An attack on a royal fortress prison in Paris

Front: When did the Storming of the Bastille occur?
Back: 14 July 1789

Front: Why was the Bastille attacked?
Back: Parisians feared a royal crackdown

Front: Why was the Bastille symbolically important?
Back: It represented royal tyranny

Front: What was the Great Fear?
Back: A wave of rural panic and attacks on nobles in 1789

Front: Why did peasants attack noble estates?
Back: Fear of aristocratic plots against them

Front: What were feudal privileges?
Back: Special rights enjoyed by the nobility

Front: When were feudal privileges abolished?
Back: August 1789

Front: What was the Declaration of the Rights of Man?
Back: A document outlining rights and freedoms

Front: What ideas influenced the Declaration?
Back: Enlightenment ideas about liberty and equality

Front: What was the Civil Constitution of the Clergy?
Back: A law placing the Church under state control

Front: Why did the Civil Constitution divide France?
Back: Many Catholics opposed state control of religion

Front: Why did Louis XVI attempt to flee France?
Back: He feared losing power

Front: What was the Flight to Varennes?
Back: Louis XVI's failed escape attempt in 1791

Front: Why did the Flight to Varennes damage the monarchy?
Back: It showed the king opposed the Revolution

Front: What political groups emerged during the Revolution?
Back: Jacobins and Girondins

Front: Who were the Jacobins?
Back: Radical revolutionaries supporting drastic change

Front: Who were the Girondins?
Back: More moderate revolutionaries

Front: What caused war in 1792?
Back: France declared war on Austria and Prussia

Front: Why did revolutionary leaders support war?
Back: They hoped to unite France and spread revolution

Front: What happened on 10 August 1792?
Back: The monarchy was overthrown

Front: What happened to Louis XVI?
Back: He was executed in January 1793

Front: Why was Louis XVI executed?
Back: He was accused of treason

Front: What was the Reign of Terror?
Back: A period of radical repression led by the Jacobins

Front: Who led the Reign of Terror?
Back: Maximilien Robespierre

Front: Why did the Terror occur?
Back: Fear of internal and external enemies

Front: What was the Committee of Public Safety?
Back: The body that governed France during the Terror

Front: What was the guillotine?
Back: A machine used for executions during the Revolution

Front: Why was the guillotine symbolic?
Back: It represented revolutionary justice

Front: What was the Law of Suspects?
Back: A law allowing arrest of suspected enemies

Front: When was the Law of Suspects passed?
What happened to many suspected enemies?
Back: They were executed

Front: What happened during the Thermidorian Reaction?
Back: Robespierre was overthrown and executed

Front: When did Robespierre fall?
What government followed the Terror?
Back: The Directory

Front: What was the Directory?
Back: A government of five directors ruling France

Front: Why was the Directory unpopular?
Back: It was seen as corrupt and ineffective

Front: What economic problems affected France?
Back: Inflation and food shortages

Front: Who rose to prominence during the Directory?
Back: Napoleon Bonaparte

Front: Why was Napoleon popular?
Back: He achieved military success

Front: What was the Coup of 18 Brumaire?
Back: Napoleon's takeover of power in 1799

Front: What government replaced the Directory?
Back: The Consulate

Front: What position did Napoleon hold?
Back: First Consul

Front: Why did Napoleon stabilise France?
Back: He restored order and reformed administration

Front: What was the Napoleonic Code?
Back: A legal system establishing equality before the law

Front: Why was the Napoleonic Code significant?
Back: It spread revolutionary principles across Europe

Front: What role did nationalism play?
Back: It increased support for revolutionary change

Front: What was the impact of the Revolution?
Back: It ended absolute monarchy in France

Front: Why was the French Revolution historically significant?
Back: It inspired political change across Europe

Front: What role did Enlightenment ideas play?
Back: They promoted liberty equality and rights

Front: What was popular sovereignty?
Back: The idea that political power belongs to the people

Front: What role did the bourgeoisie play?
Back: They led demands for political reform

Front: Why did radicalisation occur?
Back: War economic crisis and political divisions

Front: What was the significance of 1792?
Back: France became a republic

Front: What were sans culottes?
Back: Radical working class revolutionaries

Front: Why did the sans culottes influence politics?
Back: They pressured leaders to adopt radical policies

Front: What was the Cult of the Supreme Being?
Back: Robespierre's attempt to create a civic religion

Front: Why did Robespierre lose support?
Back: Fear of his increasing power

Front: What lesson did the Revolution show?
Back: Political instability can lead to dictatorship

Front: What long term impact did the Revolution have?
Back: Spread of democratic and nationalist ideas
